title: Most Crypto Trading Bots Fail — Here’s Why (From My Experience)
author: Witalson
selftext:
I’ve been working with crypto trading bots for a while now, and one thing became very clear:

**Most bots don’t fail because automation doesn’t work — they fail because of how they’re designed.**

Here are the main patterns I keep seeing.

# 1. Bots That Never Take Losses

A lot of bots look amazing on paper:

* high win rate
* smooth equity
* almost no losing trades

But when you look deeper, they simply **don’t close losses**.

Instead they:

* hold positions indefinitely
* average down
* wait for the market to come back

It works… until it doesn’t.

In real markets, trends can last much longer than expected, and you end up with:

* capital stuck in drawdown
* unrealized losses growing over time

Big red flag:

>

# 2. Grid Bots Work — Until the Market Trends

Grid bots are super popular (especially built-in exchange ones).

They:

* buy every X% down
* sell every X% up

In sideways markets, they perform pretty well.

But in strong trends:

* positions stack in one direction
* drawdown increases quickly
* risk gets out of control

The issue is the payoff structure:

>

# 3. Indicator Bots Don’t Adapt

Bots based on RSI, MACD, etc.

They often:

* work well in one market phase
* completely fail in another

Markets change. Most simple strategies don’t.

Automation just executes the same idea faster — it doesn’t fix it.

# 4. Martingale / Averaging Strategies

These increase position size after losses.

They can look stable for a long time…  
until one strong move wipes everything.

Classic case of:

>

# 5. Arbitrage / HFT Reality Check

A lot of people think arbitrage or HFT bots are “safe”.

In reality:

* you compete with firms
* latency matters
* fees kill most opportunities

For most retail traders, this edge just isn’t there.

# What Actually Makes More Sense (IMO)

From what I’ve seen, the only strategies that survive long-term are the ones that:

* accept losses
* manage risk strictly
* benefit from volatility

Trend-following is one example:

* it struggles in flat markets
* but performs when the market moves

Not perfect — but at least the math makes sense.

# Final Thought

Most bots fail not because bots are bad, but because:

* they hide risk
* they are overfitted
* they are built to look good, not survive

If you’re testing bots, one thing that helped me a lot:

>

Curious what others here are using —  
have you found anything that actually holds up over time?
